title: F-string — Variable Interpolation
domain_context: Structured logging for an ETL pipeline run
prompt: Build a log line like 'pipeline=<name> status=<status> rows=<count>' using an f-string.
hints: Prefix the string with f and wrap variable names in braces.
Three substitutions needed: {pipeline}, {status}, {rows}.
Answer shape: f'pipeline={pipeline} status={status} rows={rows}'.

title: F-string — Expression Inside Braces
domain_context: Formatting bytes-transferred metrics for a dashboard
prompt: Using one f-string, return 'Transferred 2.50 MB' by dividing bytes_transferred by 1024*1024 and formatting with two decimals.
hints: You can put an expression inside the braces, not just a variable.
Use the format spec :.2f after a colon inside the braces.
f'Transferred {bytes_transferred / 1024 / 1024:.2f} MB'.

title: List Comprehension — Extract Column Names
domain_context: Projecting a schema from a list of column metadata dicts
prompt: Given a list of column metadata dicts, return a list of just the column names.
hints: Use a list comprehension over columns.
Each element is a dict, so pull out the 'name' key.
[c['name'] for c in columns].

title: List Comprehension — Filter by Extension
domain_context: Scanning a data lake for a specific file format
prompt: Return only the .parquet file paths from the list.
hints: List comprehension with an if clause.
Use str.endswith to check the extension.
[f for f in files if f.endswith('.parquet')].
solution: [f for f in files if f.endswith('.parquet')]

title: Dict .get() — Safe Config Access
domain_context: Reading a config dict where some keys may be missing
prompt: Read the 'timeout' key from config, falling back to 30 if it is missing.
hints: Dict has a .get method that accepts a default.
config.get(key, default) returns default when key is missing.
config.get('timeout', 30).

title: Tuple Unpacking — Key/Value Pairs
domain_context: Rebuilding a config dict with uppercased keys
prompt: Given a list of (key, value) pairs, build a dict where the keys are uppercased. Use tuple unpacking in the comprehension.
hints: Dict comprehension: {expr_key: expr_value for ... in ...}.
Unpack each pair into k, v directly in the for clause.
{k.upper(): v for k, v in pairs}.
solution: {k.upper(): v for k, v in pairs}

title: Star Unpacking — Spread as Function Args
domain_context: Parameterizing a range from config values
prompt: Given a 3-element list [start, stop, step], call range() with those values and return the result as a list. Use * unpacking.
hints: range(start, stop, step) takes three positional args.
You can spread a list into positional args with *.
list(range(*steps)).

title: Ternary — Status From Row Count
domain_context: Reporting load status after an ingest job
prompt: Return 'empty' if row_count is 0, otherwise 'populated'.
hints: Python's ternary reads: <a> if <cond> else <b>.
Test row_count == 0.
'empty' if row_count == 0 else 'populated'.

title: enumerate() — 1-indexed Batch Labels
domain_context: Numbering batches for a parallel worker pool
prompt: Return labels like '1: batch_001', '2: batch_002', ... — 1-indexed. Use enumerate.
hints: enumerate yields (index, value) — pass start=1 for 1-indexing.
Combine with an f-string inside a list comprehension.
[f'{i}: {b}' for i, b in enumerate(batches, start=1)].
solution: [f'{i}: {b}' for i, b in enumerate(batches, start=1)]

title: zip() — Pair Headers With Values
domain_context: Aligning a CSV header row with a data row
prompt: Return a list of (header, value) pairs by zipping the two lists.
hints: zip takes two iterables and yields pairs.
Wrap in list() to materialize the result.
list(zip(headers, values)).

title: dict(zip()) — Record From Parallel Lists
domain_context: Building a record dict from a schema plus a row
prompt: Build a dict from the field names and row values.
hints: zip the two lists into key/value pairs.
dict() accepts an iterable of pairs.
dict(zip(fields, row)).

title: Slice — Last N Log Entries
domain_context: Tailing the most recent log lines from a buffer
prompt: Return the last 3 entries from the log buffer.
hints: Slice syntax: list[start:stop].
Negative indices count from the end.
log_entries[-3:].

title: Negative Indexing — Most Recent Record
domain_context: Peeking at the latest row in an append-only table
prompt: Return the most recent record (the last one in the list).
hints: Negative indices count from the tail.
-1 is the last element.
records[-1].

title: Star Unpacking — Prepend a Column
domain_context: Adding an id column to the front of a schema list
prompt: Return a new list with 'id' at the front followed by every column in data_cols. Use star unpacking inside the list literal.
hints: You can spread a list into a new list literal with *.
['id', *data_cols] puts 'id' first and then the rest.
['id', *data_cols].

title: sorted() — Tables By Row Count
domain_context: Ordering tables from smallest to largest
prompt: Return tables sorted ascending by 'rows'.
hints: sorted() accepts a key function.
Use a lambda that returns t['rows'].
sorted(tables, key=lambda t: t['rows']).

title: sorted() — Largest Files First
domain_context: Ranking files by disk footprint, biggest first
prompt: Return files sorted by 'size' descending.
hints: sorted() takes a reverse flag.
Combine reverse=True with a key lambda.
sorted(files, key=lambda f: f['size'], reverse=True).

title: .join() — Build a CSV Line
domain_context: Serializing a row for a CSV writer
prompt: Join the fields with commas into a single CSV line.
hints: str.join is a method on the separator, not on the list.
','.join(iterable) returns the joined string.
','.join(fields).

title: .split() — Parse a Pipe-delimited Record
domain_context: Parsing a legacy pipe-delimited export
prompt: Split the record on the pipe character and return the list of fields.
hints: str has a .split method that takes a delimiter.
Pass '|' as the delimiter.
record.split('|').

title: Membership — Column Exists in Schema
domain_context: Guarding a query against a missing column
prompt: Return True if 'email' is in the schema list.
hints: Python has a dedicated 'in' operator.
'value' in iterable returns a bool.
'email' in schema.

title: any() — Any Python Files?
domain_context: Scanning a directory listing for a file type
prompt: Return True if any file in the list ends with '.py'.
hints: any() short-circuits on the first True.
Pass it a generator that tests each element.
any(f.endswith('.py') for f in files).

title: all() — Every Record Has an ID
domain_context: Validating that every record has a required field
prompt: Return True if every record has an 'id' key.
hints: all() returns True only if every element is truthy.
Membership test 'id' in r works as the predicate.
all('id' in r for r in records).
solution: all('id' in r for r in records)

title: isinstance() — Type Guard
domain_context: Guarding a branch that only handles strings
prompt: Return True if value is a str.
hints: isinstance(obj, type) returns a bool.
The builtin type for strings is str.
isinstance(value, str).

title: set() — Deduplicate IDs
domain_context: Collapsing duplicate record ids from a noisy stream
prompt: Return the unique ids as a set.
hints: set() accepts any iterable.
Duplicates are removed automatically.
set(ids).

title: Set Intersection — Common Columns
domain_context: Comparing two schemas for overlapping fields
prompt: Return the set of columns present in both schemas.
hints: Python sets support a & b for intersection.
The result is a new set containing shared elements.
schema_a & schema_b.

title: Set Difference — Missing Columns
domain_context: Finding which required columns are absent from a table
prompt: Return the set of columns present in 'required' but missing from 'actual'.
hints: The - operator on sets computes the difference.
a - b keeps elements in a that are not in b.
required - actual.

title: map() — Uppercase All Names
domain_context: Normalizing a batch of user names for matching
prompt: Apply str.upper to every element of names and return a list.
hints: map(func, iterable) returns a map object — wrap in list().
str.upper is a method you can pass as a function.
list(map(str.upper, names)).

title: filter() — Drop None Values
domain_context: Keeping only rows that were parsed successfully
prompt: Return the values that are not None, as a list.
hints: filter(pred, iterable) keeps elements where pred returns truthy.
A lambda like v is not None distinguishes None from 0.
list(filter(lambda v: v is not None, values)).
solution: list(filter(lambda v: v is not None, values))

title: .startswith() — Prefix Route Check
domain_context: Routing a file by its storage prefix
prompt: Return True if path starts with 's3://'.
hints: str has a .startswith method that returns a bool.
Pass the prefix string as the argument.
path.startswith('s3://').

title: os.getenv() — Config With Default
domain_context: Reading a timeout from the environment with a fallback
prompt: Read PYPECKER_TIMEOUT from the environment, defaulting to '30' if the variable is unset.
hints: os.getenv(name, default) returns the default when the var is missing.
Strings only — env values are always strings.
os.getenv('PYPECKER_TIMEOUT', '30').

title: len() — Counts Across Types
domain_context: Reporting quick stats about a record batch
prompt: Return a list [record count, schema column count, message length] — three len() calls in one expression.
hints: len works on lists, dicts, and strings.
Build a list literal of three len() calls.
[len(records), len(schema), len(msg)].
